content:
Hey all, thinking about my next step of Rust-for-grub work and I wanted to confirm my understanding of what you all do on Linux.

Like Linux, grub also has modules that use symbols exported from a core kernel.

Looking at https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/blob/rust/rust/exports.c , it seems like you generate exports for a bunch of things automatically. So if a module wants to call something on Vec<>, it ends up going via the core kernel, right?

Does that mean you end up with a larger kernel because you can't prune out parts of the core library that aren't called (because you don't know what a module will end up using)? I ask because I'm pondering whether or not to build the core into the kernel or build the parts of the core used by each module into each module.

I think the core crate is bigger than the current grub kernel so I'm a bit loathe to propose including the whole thing! On the other hand the idea of modules compiled _after_ the primary build process isn't really a thing in grub-land so it might be possible to trim it down more aggressively.

So far all the APIs we support are places there are existing kernel APIs for, and we expose C-ABI functions from Rust, and function-pointer structs (e.g. file_operations).

Which is to say the kernel C code never calls any Vec<T> methods, those are called exclusively from Rust code.

I'm not sure if that answers your question?

So if a module wants to call something on Vec<>, it ends up going via the core kernel, right?

Yes, core, alloc, etc. go into the "shared" Rust support.

Does that mean you end up with a larger kernel because you can't prune out parts of the core library that aren't called (because you don't know what a module will end up using)?

Yeah, but you can also see it the other way around: it allows us to have a smaller kernel, since the idea is that eventually many modules will reuse the same code.

In order to take care of the "rest" that we do not expect anybody using (or that we do not want anybody to use), we are also trimming alloc in our fork (no collections, no sync, no rc...), and we also requested upstream a few "modularization" options for core (some already in, e.g. no_fp_fmt_parse).
A lot of the "worst" offenders for codesize are generics, which only get codegen'd if they're actually used, so that helps.

Cool.

Yeah, I was thinking of Rust modules calling Rust core functionality.

I'll have a crack again at some point (work keeps wanting me to Create Shareholder Value, unfortunately) and let you know how I go. I suspect I might go down the path of forking and trimming core and alloc, but I'll see how I go.

A lot of the "worst" offenders for codesize are generics, which only get codegen'd if they're actually used, so that helps.

IIUC you're saying Vec<u8>::whatever is only generated if there's actually a Vec<u8>. Is that right? How would the compiler be able to reason about that if there could be an out-of-tree module that ends up trying to call Vec<u8>::foo?

When compiling core (or any crate for that matter) rustc emits the IR for generics methods that can be read by rustc when compiling something downstream and the method will be code-gen'd then if it wasn't already codegen'd inside core.
